---
title: "Crimes Against Causality"
---

# Welcome {.unnumbered}

> *"The data never speaks for itself. If the data starts speaking to you, consider seeing a doctor."*
> — Wilbert Wright

Every day, confident claims are made in the name of science. A chart shows that
a music genre is deadlier than war. An algorithm flags the buildings most likely
to burn. A newspaper names the hospital most likely to kill you. Most of these
claims are built on real data, honestly collected — and most of them are wrong.

This is a book of **crimes against causality**: cases where valid-looking
evidence leads to an invalid conclusion. Each case is a puzzle. You are handed
the crime scene, the exhibits, and a list of questions. Your job is to find the
fatal flaw before the solution reveals it.

Work the cases like a detective. Look at who is *missing* from the data. Ask what
the chart is actually measuring. Notice when an impressive number answers the
wrong question. The skills you build here — spotting selection effects, base-rate
traps, confounding, and reversed conditional probabilities — are the same skills
that separate trustworthy evidence from compelling nonsense.

::: {.callout-tip}
## How this book is organised
- **Introduction** — how to read the cases, the world they are set in, and the
  two detectives who investigate them.
- **The Cases** — start with the [overview table](cases/overview.qmd), then dive
  into any case that catches your eye.
- **The Solutions** — the answers, what went wrong, background, and further
  reading. Each case links straight to its solution (and back).
- **References & Index** — a combined bibliography and a tag index for finding
  cases by topic, fallacy, or difficulty.
:::

Read on, detective. The first crime scene is waiting.
